architecture_style: Doric peripteral temple; sculptural programme by Phidias workshop
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Temple of Athena Parthenos crowning the Acropolis. Ongoing conservation scaffolding shifts with each campaign.
facts: Some sculptures are displayed in the Acropolis Museum while casts occupy pediments on site.
Summer midday heat makes dawn or late tickets more comfortable.
history: Periclean building programme replaced older temples destroyed by the Persians; later conversions included church and mosque phases.
significance: Canonical image of Classical Greece and democratic-city identity.

architecture_style: Roman stone theatre with restored stage house
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Stone Roman odeon restored for summer Athens Epidaurus Festival performances under the lit Acropolis rock face.
facts: Named for the wealthy sophist Herodes Atticus who funded it in memory of his wife.
Seating is marble; cushions are allowed on rented seats for some shows.
history: Ruined roof and upper cavea were rebuilt in the 20th century to revive large-scale music and drama.
significance: The romantic night shot every visitor recognises from the path up to the Propylaea.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Civic heart where assemblies met, shops lined stoas, and philosophers taught. Hephaisteion temple rises intact on the hillock.
facts: Combined tickets often bundle Acropolis slopes with the agora on the same day.
The Stoa of Attalos houses a small museum of finds.
history: Classical democracy's open-air offices; Roman rebuilds followed sacks and earthquakes.
significance: Walkable archaeology linking myth, democracy, and daily commerce.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: American School reconstruction housing agora small finds—pottery, ballots, weights—with shaded upper walkway views.
facts: Gift of Attalus II of Pergamon; modern rebuild used inscriptions and foundations as guides.
Air-conditioned galleries offer summer relief before returning to sunlit ruins.
history: Original stoa displayed royal patronage; the modern copy proves controversial yet didactic for visitors.
significance: Best place to connect objects to the rubble plan below your feet.

year_built: 330 BC limestone seats; marble 144 AD; modern restoration 19th c.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: All-marble stadium that hosted the 1896 Olympic revival. Audio guides explain starting line grooves and royal boxes.
facts: You can jog the track on a quiet morning for a small fee.
The tunnel mimics ancient athlete procession routes.
history: Lycurgus-era limestone tiers were clad in Herodes Atticus marble; excavators cleared silt after centuries of burial.
significance: Physical link between ancient Panathenaia festival and modern Olympic narrative.

architecture_style: Modern glass and concrete over archaeological site
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Purpose-built museum for Parthenon sculptures and slope finds, with views up to the Acropolis rock.
facts: Glass floors reveal excavations under the entrance.
The top gallery aligns with the Parthenon across the skyline.
history: Replaced cramped hilltop rooms; the design debates still shape discussions on repatriation of marbles.
significance: Essential context before or after climbing the Acropolis.

year_built: 6th c. BC start; completed 2nd c. AD (Hadrian)
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Ruined sanctuary once housing a colossal chryselephantine cult image; a few towering columns remain.
facts: Hadrian's Arch stands beside the fence marking old and new city.
Combined tickets may include the site with other central ruins.
history: Peisistratid foundations waited centuries for Roman completion under Hadrian.
significance: Scale reference for imperial Roman Athens.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Flagship collection of Greek antiquity from prehistory through late Roman bronzes and frescoes.
facts: Mask of Agamemnon and Antikythera mechanism are star exhibits.
Café courtyard offers shade before upper-floor vases.
history: Founded to house growing national excavations after independence.
significance: The single best overview of Greek material culture.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Flea market streets, souvlaki counters, and rooftop bars with Acropolis views around Monastiraki station.
facts: Sunday mornings are busiest for antiques and bric-a-brac.
Metro exhibits behind glass line the ticket hall.
history: Named for a small monastery demolished in the 18th century; the square stayed a commercial hinge.
significance: Transit hub between Plaka, Psiri, and the ancient Agora.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Marble octagon combining sundials, water clock, and wind vane personifications on each side.
facts: Each relief names a classical wind direction.
Entry is often bundled with Roman Agora tickets.
history: Attributed to Andronikos of Kyrrhos; among the best-preserved ancient timekeeping structures.
significance: Unique fusion of meteorology, astronomy, and civic pride.

architecture_style: Roman triumphal arch with Corinthian columns
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Marble gateway marking the boundary between old city of Theseus and Hadrian's newer Athens.
facts: Inscriptions face both directions with different dedications.
Illuminated at night beside busy tram lines.
history: Erected to celebrate Hadrian's benefactions including the nearby Olympeion completion.
significance: Readable symbol of imperial Roman urban expansion.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Parliament frontage where Evzones perform the changing of the guard at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier.
facts: Hourly ceremony draws crowds; arrive early for shade in summer.
Metro lines cross beneath the square.
history: Named after the 1843 constitution granted after popular demonstration at the palace gates.
significance: Modern Athens' default protest and celebration plaza.
